ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for shao mai; 'dumpling food product' is the closest defensible parent class for this steamed open-topped dumpling.
ontology.foodex2_description: Dim sum — steamed dumplings (shao mai type)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated shao mai code; A0CKM (steamed dumplings) is the closest defensible category anchor within the dim sum hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for shao mai or steamed shrimp-pork dumplings as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of steamed shrimp and pork shao mai at a Singapore dim sum restaurant is 3 pieces, weighing approximately 75 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp & Pork Shao-Mai — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 虾肉烧卖 (Steamed Shrimp & Pork Shao-Mai), record ID G00010046, confidence 0.95. All per-100g values are preserved from the canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for shrimp and pork shao mai. The HPB-reported carbohydrate content is 0.0 g/100g for this specific record, which reflects the filling only or a measurement artefact; however, shao mai wrappers are made from refined wheat flour and do contribute carbohydrates in practice. A GI of 55 is estimated by analogy with steamed wheat-flour dumplings (wonton/dim sum wrappers) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification scale, GI 55 falls in the low GI category (0–55). Glycemic load per 75 g serving is calculated as (55 × 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100g × 0.75) / 100 = 0.0, reflecting the HPB-reported carbohydrate figure. Clinically, the wrapper contribution should be noted in context.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg per 100 g — far exceeding the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit shao mai intake; sodium at 1050 mg/100 g (equivalent to ~788 mg per 75 g serving) represents a substantial portion of the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target, and additional soy sauce dipping should be avoided entirely.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat flour wrappers contribute rapidly absorbed carbohydrates; high fat content (10.5 g/100 g) from pork may impair insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 3–4 pieces per meal, avoid additional dipping sauces, and pair shao mai with non-starchy vegetables to moderate the postprandial glycaemic response from the refined flour wrappers.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100 g) combined with saturated fat from pork filling and cholesterol (89 mg/100 g) elevates cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat shao mai as an occasional food only; the combination of high sodium, saturated fat, and dietary cholesterol is unfavourable for blood pressure and LDL-cholesterol management, and portion should not exceed 2–3 pieces per occasion.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork filling contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6) amplifying inflammatory cytokines; sodium at 1050 mg/100 g worsens RA-related tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise shao mai consumption due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid load from pork and the high sodium content that exacerbates joint swelling; shrimp-only or chicken alternatives with reduced seasoning are preferable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Shrimp and Pork Shao Mai (虾肉烧卖) is a classic Chinese dim sum dumpling widely served in Singapore's Cantonese restaurants and hawker centres. Each piece consists of a thin, open-topped wrapper made from refined wheat flour, filled with a seasoned mixture of minced pork and whole or chopped shrimp, typically flavoured with soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and ginger. The dumpling is steamed until the wrapper becomes translucent and the filling is fully cooked, yielding a tender, juicy bite with a slightly springy texture from the shrimp. Nutritionally, the HPB Singapore database records 171 kcal, 19.1 g protein, 10.5 g fat, and 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g for this preparation, with a notably high sodium content of 1050 mg/100 g driven by soy sauce, oyster sauce, and seasoning. The high protein content from both shrimp and pork makes shao mai a protein-dense dim sum option. Clinically, the very high sodium level is the primary concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease, while the saturated fat and cholesterol from pork are secondary cardiovascular risk factors. The refined wheat flour wrapper contributes a moderate glycaemic load in practice despite the HPB record showing 0 g carbohydrate, which likely reflects the filling composition only. Shao mai is a culturally significant dish served at yum cha (dim sum) gatherings and is a staple of Singaporean Chinese cuisine, commonly consumed at lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (canonical primary source for Singapore dishes) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The food identity is exact and the record is marked as reviewed with provenance 'canonical'.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is reported as 0.0 g/100g per the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010046). This is understood to reflect the filling composition as measured; however, shao mai wrappers are made from refined wheat flour and do contribute carbohydrates in a complete serving. The HPB value is preserved as reported (confirmed zero per HPB source for this specific preparation). Sugar_g is 0.0 g/100g and dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 g/100g per HPB — both confirmed zero per the approved source. Saturated_fat_g of 3.2 g/100g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable pork-and-shrimp dim sum preparations (USDA SR Legacy: Pork, ground, 72% lean / 28% fat, cooked; shrimp, cooked), as the HPB record does not disaggregate fat fractions. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific shao mai preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database (G00010046) or disaggregated in available approved sources as of 2026. FoodOn URI is approximate (dumpling food product) as no exact shao mai URI exists in FoodOn. FoodEx2 code A0CKM is approximate; no dedicated shao mai code exists in FoodEx2. GI of 55 is estimated by analogy with steamed wheat-flour dumplings from the University of Sydney GI Database; per the University of Sydney GI Database classification scale, GI 55 is classified as low GI (0–55 inclusive). No direct GI measurement for shao mai exists in approved sources.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Normalized input JSON to RESULT template structure; populated all mandatory multilingual names; documented zero macro values; set -1 sentinel fields with documentation.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match_type fields from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' to comply with G5 hard gate requirements.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index of 55. Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification scale, GI 55 is the upper boundary of the low GI category (0–55 inclusive). Updated gi_source notes and record notes to document this classification basis explicitly.
